Officer Involved In Incident: Jeffrey Masek
Volunteer Notes: The officer was caught during an undercover operation, so his actions were not against a citizen
text: Hennepin Co. deputy charged with child sex  crimes  Criminal charges detail how Masek allegedly began an inappropriate conversation with what he  thought was a 15-year-old girl.     Author: KARE Staff   Published: 4/30/2018 8:40:28 PM   Updated: 9:51 AM CDT May 1, 2018   MINNEAPOLIS - A Hennepin County deputy is on administrative leave after being charged  with three child sex crimes.  According to court records in Barron County, Wisconsin, 60-year-old Jeffrey Masek was  charged Monday with three felonies: using a computer to facilitate a child sex crime, child  enticement-sexual contact, and attempted second-degree sexual assault of a child.  A criminal complaint states Masek was caught during an undercover operation, where a Rice  Lake police officer placed a personal ad looking for a sexual relationship on a website, using the  persona of a girl under the age of 16.   On March 22, she received an email off that personal ad, from a man who described himself as  an experienced, single white man that was a professional in their early 50s. The email went into  graphic detail and requested to share photos. It was signed, "Thanks Jeff."  A few more emails were shared, some including nude photos of a white man, and the two  exchanged phone numbers. A search of the number given was registered to a 60-year-old Jeffrey  Jon Masek, from Polk County, Wis. or Cottage Grove, Minn.   A text conversation, which is included in the criminal charges, details how the undercover  officer, posing as a young girl, told the man she was "almost 16" and him responding that the  youngest he's been with was 18. He also described himself as an aeronautical engineer "at the  Phd level."   At one point, the officer asked the man if he's ever talked to other 15-year-old girls and he  responded no. He stated, "Honestly, it scares the heck out of me" due to "legal reasons."   The two planned to meet at Farm and Fleet, where the man said he'd be driving a gold 1999  GMC Sierra. In a check of vehicles registered to Masek, the officer learned he owned a vehicle  of the same year, make and color.   At 6 p.m., April 28, a vehicle matching the description of Masek's entered the Cedar Mall  parking lot and the undercover officer received a text message stating, "I'm here."  Officers took Masek into custody and while he was being arrested, he allegedly said, "This is the  worst day in my life." He also advised officers he was a Hennepin County Sheriff's deputy.   In an interview with authorities, Masek admitted sending the graphic text messages but said the  only reason he showed up for the meeting was that he thought the girl would find someone else  and be preyed on and he would end up reading about a dead body, according to the charges.   When asked if law enforcement would find any other communication with minors, he admitted  he was speaking wih a possible 12-year-old but stopped when he became suspicious it might be a  sting operation.   Masek appeared in court on Monday. His preliminary hearing is scheduled for June 4.  The Hennepin County Sheriff's Office provided KARE 11 with the following statement:  "The Hennepin County Sheriff's Office has learned that Deputy Jeffrey Jon Masek has been  arrested on criminal charges in Rice Lake, Wisconsin, and is being held in custody in the Barron  County Jail in Barron, Wisconsin. Masek has been placed on Administrative Leave pending  internal investigation. The criminal charges are being investigated by Rice Lake Police."

Officer Involved In Incident: Myron Wieland
text: New Ulm police chief pleas to a misdemeanor charge   By Nancy Madsen nmadsen@mankatofreepress.com   Dec 17, 2015  https://www.mankatofreepress.com/news/local_news/new-ulm-police-chief-pleas-to-a- misdemeanor-charge/article_389c96fe-a504-11e5-b2d3-e3551c172bcf.html  NEW ULM — New Ulm Police Chief Myron Wieland pleaded guilty Thursday to a  misdemeanor as part of an agreement dealing with charges from a domestic incident in August.  Wieland will spend a year on probation and pay a $300 fine, said Watonwan County Attorney  Stephen Lindee, who prosecuted the case. There is a stay of adjudication on the charge and  Wieland faces no additional jail time under sentencing by Nicollet County Judge Todd Westphal  at Brown County District Court. After a year, the charge will be dismissed.  Lindee said the case drew more scrutiny because of Wieland's public position.  "This agreement was approved by the victim and the judge said today that it is in line with how  things usually go, given that this is a first-time offender with no prior criminal history," Lindee  said.  Wieland, 54, had been charged with two counts of domestic assault and a count of disorderly  conduct, all misdemeanors. He was arrested after an incident precipitated by an argument over  money with his wife that took place at his house Aug. 24.  His wife said Wieland threw her against a wall then placed his hands around her neck and threw  her on the bed, according to the criminal complaint. At that point she told Wieland to get out of  the house or she would call 911. He stopped assaulting her at that point and she left the house.  An order for Wieland to live outside their home had been lifted in a previous court appearance.  To avoid a conflict of interest, Wieland was housed in Faribault County Jail and his case was  investigated by Washington County Sheriff's Department.  City Manager Brian Gramentz said Wieland will remain on administrative leave while the city  seeks guidance from the Minnesota Police Officers Standards and Training Board about the  police chief's license. Gramentz said he expects a fairly quick response from the board.  "We need to verify that he can or cannot maintain his license," he said.  In August, the standards coordinator for the Police Officers Standards and Training Board said  that if Wieland is convicted of certain types of misdemeanors, he will be required to go before  the POST Complaint Committee for a license review.  Wieland has been with the New Ulm Police Department for 28 years, serving as a patrol officer,  sergeant and commander before becoming chief Feb. 1, 2009.  New Ulm police chief to be suspended two weeks   By Nancy Madsen nmadsen@mankatofreepress.com   Dec 22, 2015  https://www.mankatofreepress.com/news/local_news/new-ulm-police-chief-to-be-suspended- two-weeks/article_719cc574-a8f5-11e5-a4e7-47c90dd79eee.html?mode=jqm  NEW ULM — New Ulm Police Chief Myron Wieland is suspended for two weeks without pay  as the result of a guilty plea last week to misdemeanor disorderly conduct.  The guilty plea Thursday came from an agreement dealing with charges from a domestic incident  in August. He has been on administrative leave from the city police department since the  incident.  "After applying applicable city policies to this situation, a determination was made that Police  Chief Myron Wieland, violated the Professional Conduct of Peace Officers Policy and was  issued a two-week suspension without pay," the city said in a news release issued Tuesday  afternoon.  The decision came after the city's personnel committee reviewed the records. City Manager  Brian Gramentz said on Tuesday morning that a meeting with Wieland would also take place.  Wieland will face no license review from the Minnesota Police Officers Standards and Training  Board.

text: By David Olson July 24, 2003 11:00 PM  We are part of The Trust Project. Larry Costello said Thursday he surrendered his peace officer license and will never again hold a job in law enforcement. The former Clay County sheriff sent the Minnesota Board of Peace Officer Standards and Training he was giving up his license in a letter sent to the agency several days ago. The Post Board's complaint committee had been scheduled to discuss Costello's license status at a meeting Thursday. Citing data privacy law, board officials wouldn't say whether the hearing happened. "It's moot," said Costello, who took disability retirement in May 2002 for a bad back. He was slated to receive $60,676 per year for the disability. In June, Costello pleaded guilty in Clay County District Court to a charge of misconduct of a public officer. He admitted violating a Minnesota law in 2001 when he spent public funds to purchase six two-way radios for personal use. Costello gives up law license - InForum | Fargo, Moorhead and West Fa... https://www.inforum.com/newsmd/costello-gives-up-law-license 1 of 3 11/22/2022, 2:31 AM He was ordered to do 100 hours of community service work and was fined $345. Immediately after he retired, Costello said he notified the standards board that his license was inactive. He also said he never planned to find another job in law enforcement, which would mean the loss of his disability benefits. Costello said he thought the step meant his license was dead. He was surprised, he said, when he got a letter from the standards board several weeks ago inviting him to attend Thursday's meeting of the complaint committee, at which his licensure would be discussed. Costello said he responded with his letter officially surrendering his license. Future employment in a field other than law enforcement remains a possibility, Costello said. But because of poor health he has no plans at this time to pursue a new career. "I'm not getting any better." Costello said the hearing on his license was scheduled after he pleaded guilty to the misconduct charge in June. Board officials can't comment on matters that come before the complaint committee until action has been taken by the full board. Information can be released once a settlement and consent order is reached, said Mary Bjornberg, the board's assistant director. The 100 hours of community service he was ordered to do should be completed by the end of the weekend, Costello said. Costello is satisfying his obligation by volunteering with the Dilworth Lions Club, something he has been doing since 1985. A federal lawsuit remains pending against Costello. text: Amelia Huffman  Interim Chief of Police  Minneapolis Police Department  350 South 5th Street, Room 130  Minneapolis, MN 55415  (612) 673-3550  Amelia.Huffman@minneapolismn.gov  TO:  CC:  DATE:  RE:  Officer Troy Dillard  Office of Police Conduct Review  May 12th, 2022  OPCR Case #21-08926  CHIEF'S DECISION:  ❑Discharge  KSuspension  Duration:  KPermanent Demotion  KTemporary Demotion, Duration:  Written Reprimand  K No Merit  This memo summarizes my approach and reasoning for my decision as Chief of Police to issue a letter of  reprimand. This memo accompanies the enclosed MPD Notice of Discipline letter.  Policies Violated:  3-801  (III)(A)(1)  Off-Duty Employment  B  Sustained  13.43 - Personnel Data  4-223  3-801  (IV)(A)(6)  (III)(A)(20)  BWC Activation  Off-duty Sign-on  B  B  Sustained  Sustained  Summary of the Basis for Decision:  The facts noted in the case investigation support the conclusion that Officer Dillard violated Minneapolis  Police Department policies.  •  On 8/22/21, a complaint was received in the Office of Police Conduct Review and the  investigator was initially unable to determine the identity of Minneapolis Police Department  1942497 officers working off-duty jobs at the venue. During the investigation of that complaint, it was  found that Officer Dillard was working off-duty at The Armory.  •  According to MPD records, Officer Dillard had not submitted an off-duty employment request  and was not approved to work at this off-duty location as required by policy  •  13.43 - Personnel Data  •  According to MECC records, Officer Dillard failed to contact MECC to log-on with an off-duty call  sign. This left no record in the dispatch system that Officer Dillard was working at this location  at this time. Furthermore, had he needed assistance from other officers, his location would not  be known to dispatchers.  •  Officer Dillard had in-person contact with the complainant who was in an adversarial situation  with civilian site security. He did not activate his Body Worn Camera during this interaction.  Officer Dillard stated that he was never involved with the incident that resulted in the  complaint. He noted that he was present with Lt. McCree and observed security dealing with  the incident from about 6 feet away. Lt. McCree indicated in his interview that he had been  called by security to assist in dealing with the incident.  •  At his Loudermill hearing, Officer Dillard acknowledged making a couple of mistakes in this  matter.  As the interim Chief of Police, I am responsible for providing clear expectations for what is acceptable  behavior in our workplaces as well as what will not be tolerated. The Police Conduct Review panel  recommended a finding of merit for four policy violations in this case, and I concur with their  recommendation. While the individual violations of the off-duty work policies alone are primarily  administrative, together they undermine the Department's ability to hold officers accountable for their  conduct while working in the Minneapolis Department uniform and with the authority of the  Minneapolis Police Department. Similarly, proper body worn camera usage is an important vehicle for  accountability. I hold officers of the Minneapolis Police Department to a high standard and expect them  to demonstrate our department's core values of trust, accountability and professional service. Officer  Dillard's violations in this matter undermine accountability and public trust.  As interim Chief of Police with authority to discipline for violations of policy under Minn. Stat. §626.89  Subd. 17, I am issuing a written reprimand to Officer Dillard for his failure to obtain approval prior to  working at this off-duty site, his failure to contact MECC to obtain log in with a call sign at the start of  off-duty work and his failure to activate his BWC.  13.43 - Personnel Data  DocuSigned by:  Andz4 H÷444•  FBFE4338427B46A... text: Police Department – Brian O'Hara,  Chief of Police  350 S. Fifth St. - Room 130  Minneapolis, MN 55415  TEL  612.673.3559                TO:  Rebecca Madich, Badge 5867  CC:  Office of Police Conduct Review   DATE:  January 25, 2023  RE:  Administrative Investigation Case #20-05313     CHIEF'S DECISION:  Written Reprimand        This memo summarizes my approach and reasoning for my decision as Chief of Police that the listed policy  violations by Officer Madich is sustained.  The memo accompanies the enclosed Notice of Discipline Form.     Summary of the Basis for Decision:     Allegation #1:    It is alleged that Officer Madich failed to follow the Domestic Protocol when she did not complete a  Domestic Abuse Report on May 8, 2020.  MPD P/P 7-314(IV)(A)(6) Domestic Abuse.  x  On March 8, 2020 Officer Madich and her partner (now separated from employment) responded  to a 17 year old who stated that she was assaulted by her husband, who just left the scene.    x  Officer Madich noted in VisiNet and informed investigators that she had dealt with this victim on a  previous domestic incident (February 6, 2020) and arranged for the victim to find safe housing,  which was difficult because the victim is a juvenile.  x  In this incident, Officer Madich stated that she believes she contacted "La Case De Esperanza" in St.  Paul, as they assist Latino women in need of housing, and believe that they may come and pick-up  the victim.  However, she did not wait with the victim/complainant until they arrived.  x  Officer Madich the failed to document the Domestic Assault in PIMS as required by MPD P/P  7/314(IV)(A)(6).  x  The Police Conduct Review Panel issued a determination of Merit to this allegation.  x  I concur with the Police Conduct Review Panel that there is Merit to this allegation and that there  is a preponderance of the evidence to prove that Officer Madich failed to follow the Domestic  Protocol.  This allegation is Sustained.    The facts noted in the case investigation support the conclusion that Officer Madich violated Minneapolis  Police Department Policy 7-314(IV)(A)(6). I concur with the recommendation from the Police Conduct  Review Panel that there is Merit to this allegation and the allegation is Sustained.    As Chief of Police, I am responsible for providing clear expectations about behavior and conduct in all  situations, not just when it is convenient.  These expectations extend from myself to the command staff  and all levels of the Minneapolis Police Department.  My predecessors have also borne responsibility for  setting expectations, and it is incumbent on every Chief to hold officers accountable for breeches of those  expectations.  Legitimacy and reputation are of critical importance to police officers.  Officers carry a badge  and gun and are called upon to make some of the hardest decisions possible.  It takes countless actions to  build a reservoir of trust, and only one inaction to completely drain it.  We cannot afford to lose legitimacy  with the people we serve.      I hold officers of the Minneapolis Police Department to a high standard.  I expect them to live up to our  oath of office, our professional code of ethics and our departments core values of trust, accountability and  professional service.  Furthermore, I expect them to demonstrate procedural justice in their work. text: STAR TRIBUNE ›  Annotations   Friends of motorcyclist killed in collision with Minneapolis police vehicle seek answers MATT MCKINNEY MAY ೄೈ, ೅ೃೄೆ Riding his motorcycle down Blaisdell Avenue S. in Minneapolis on Friday, his girlfriend seated behind him, Ivan Romero Olivares had money in his pocket and a plan: it was Mother's Day in Mexico, and he was on his way to wire cash to his mother before calling. In a split second, everything changed for the -year-old baker, who was widely known as Ivan Romero. A police SUV rushing along W. th Street to a deadly shooting in Uptown pulled across Blaisdell. Romero struck the rear passenger side of the vehicle and ﬂew off the Yamaha, landing on the pavement. He died instantly, according to an autopsy report issued Tuesday. His girlfriend, Joselin Torrejon-Villamil, survived. e deadly chain of events has shaken Romero's friends, including Oscar Ramirez, who said Tuesday he's still looking for answers from police. "I feel like the police try to make excuses," he said, referring to a Minneapolis Police Department statement issued a day after the collision that said the police vehicle was traveling below the speed limit and had its ﬂashing lights and siren on. "We want to know the truth." e Police Department has not said who had the green light in the collision, though three witnesses to the crash, two of whom gave statements to the police, told the Star Tribune that the police squad went through a red light. Two of those witnesses estimated the police vehicle's speed at  mph to  ✕ mph as it passed vehicles heading west on W. th Street. A third witness said she couldn't estimate the police vehicle's speed, saying she didn't get a good look. Minneapolis police officers surveyed the scene where a police vehicle and motorcycle collided at the intersection of Blaisdell and th in Minneapolis, Min., Friday, May , . e police statement said witnesses, accident reconstruction evidence and video show the squad car was traveling "well below the posted speed limit," but police officials have said they could not comment further or release the video because the investigation was continuing. Authorities also have been reluctant to offer many details of the shooting that preceded the crash by half an hour, saying that they wanted to continue gathering facts and not respond to speculation. According to a Saturday news release, Terrance T. Franklin, , died during a struggle with officers in a basement at  Bryant Av. S. Police said that they were chasing Franklin because of a tip he had committed an earlier burglary and that the shots were ﬁred after he tried to grab an officer's machine pistol. Two officers were wounded, but police haven't described who ﬁred the fatal shots or whether the officers and Franklin were shot with the machine pistol. Franklin's family members also have called on police to explain how he died. On Tuesday, Mayor R.T. Rybak, defended Police Chief Janeé Harteau's handling of the case, saying it was important to get a thorough understanding of the incident before speaking publicly. "is is an incredibly serious, tragic incident that has taken a life and deeply upset many people," Rybak said. "Our hearts go out to this family. We recognize how signiﬁcant it is. at's why we're doing everything we can to understand what happened." Ramirez said he met Romero several years ago when they both worked for the New French Bakery. Romero, originally from Morales, Mexico, didn't have many relatives in Minnesota. He and Ramirez bought motorcycles and sometimes took longer trips through western Wisconsin. Last year, Romero bought the sleek, black Yamaha he was riding when he died. "He was like a baby with a new toy," said Benjamin Garcia, another friend of Romero's. "He was a great friend; a hard worker," Ramirez said, adding that it was Romero's uncle who had to call his parents in Morales. "at was their only boy, so they are really heartbroken," Ramirez said. text: Amelia Huffman  Interim Chief of Police  Minneapolis Police Department  350 South 5th Street, Room 130  Minneapolis, MN 55415  (612) 673-3550  Amelia.Huffman@minneapolismn.gov  TO: Juan Alonzo Jr., Badge 0077  CC: Office of Police Conduct Review  DATE: September 17, 2022  RE: Administrative Investigation Case #20-12979  CHIEF'S DECISION:  E Discharge  E Suspension  Duration:  E Permanent Demotion  E Temporary Demotion, Duration:  Written Reprimand  E No Merit  0  This memo summarizes my approach and reasoning for my decision as Chief of Police that the listed  policy violations by Officer Alonzo Jr. are sustained. The memo accompanies the enclosed Notice of  Discipline.  Allegations:  Policy Number  Sub-Section  Policy Description  Category  Disposition  7-402  (III)(A)  Pursuit Policy  B  Sustained  7-402  (IV)(B)(2)  Pursuit Policy  B  Sustained  13.43 - Personnel Data  Summary of the Basis for Decision:  The facts noted in the case investigation support the conclusion that Officer Alonzo Jr. violated the two  Minneapolis Police Department Policies related to pursuit  I concur with the recommendations from the Police Conduct Review Panel in this decision.  violations.  13.43  13.43 - Personnel Data  1926741 Allegation #1  •  Officer Alonzo was the passenger when he and his partner observed two vehicles racing and  driving in a reckless manner.  •  Officers attempted to stop the vehicle by activating their emergency lights and sirens, but the  vehicle did not stop.  •  The vehicle continued to drive in what officers described as a flagrantly reckless manner, nearly  causing multiple accidents.  •  Both officers admitted to there being confusion about whether to pursue or stop, but ultimately  the pursuit continued until the fleeing vehicle crashed and the occupants fled on foot.  •  The Police Conduct Review Panel (PCRP) recommended that there is merit to this investigation.  •  I agree with the recommendation of the PCRP. The preponderance of the evidence shows that  Officer Alonzo, though the passenger, continued this pursuit while the driving conduct of the  fleeing vehicle posed a danger to the public. Officer Alonzo did not articulate specific facts to  support the necessity of engaging in the pursuit despite the danger. This allegation is Sustained.  Allegation #2  •  Officer Alonzo was the passenger during this pursuit, and it was his responsibility to follow MPD  policy related to the role of officers in a pursuit.  •  Officer Alonzo aired that they were not pursuing, but when they continued the pursuit, did not  update dispatch with any of the required information about the pursuit (location, speed,  direction of travel and reason for the pursuit).  •  The Police Conduct Review Panel recommended that there is merit to this allegation.  •  I agree with the recommendation of the PCRP. The preponderance of the evidence shows that  Officer Alonzo failed to follow the requirements described in policy for officers involved in  pursuits. This allegation is Sustained.  13.43 - Personnel Data  As the interim Chief of Police, I am responsible for providing clear expectations for what is acceptable  behavior in our workplaces as well as what will not be tolerated. The Police Conduct Review panel  recommended a finding of merit for two  1 3.43  policy violations in this case, and I concur  with their recommendations.  1926742 I hold officers of the Minneapolis Police Department to a high standard. I expect them to live up to our  oath of office, our professional code of ethics and our department's core values of trust, accountability  and professional service. Furthermore, I expect them to demonstrate a commitment to preserving  safety and the sanctity of life. With this conduct, Officer Alonzo failed to meet MPD standards. He  allowed a dangerous pursuit to continue absent specific, articulable facts which would necessitate the  pursuit despite the danger. Additionally, he failed to comply with policy requirements that officers in  pursuits air information critical for dispatch, other officers and the pursuit supervisor to have. The  violations in this matter undermine public safety.  As interim Chief of Police with authority to discipline for violations of policy under Minn. Stat 626.89  Subd. 17, I am issuing a Letter of Reprimand to Officer Alonzo for his failure to follow the pursuit policy.  13.43 - Personnel Data  DocuSigned by:  c mmm Ait4tA. H44to.m•  Amelia Huffman  Interim Chief of Police  CC:  OPCR Case File  Inspector Blackwell  Enclosure: Notice of Discipline Form  1926743

text: Police Department – Brian O'Hara,  Chief of Police  350 S. Fifth St. ­ Room 130  Minneapolis, MN 55415  TEL  612.673.3559                TO:  Zakari Ketchmark, Badge #3682  CC:  Office of Police Conduct Review   DATE:  July 14, 2023  RE:  Administrative Investigation Case #22­03933      CHIEF'S DECISION:  Letter of Reprimand      This memo summarizes my approach and reasoning for my decision as Chief of Police that the listed policy  violation by Officer Ketchmark is sustained.  The memo accompanies the enclosed Notice of Discipline  form.     Summary of the Basis for Decision:     Allegation #1:  MPD P&P 10­401(IV)(B) Responsibility to Inventory Property and Evidence    On October 31, 2021 Officer Ketchmark was in the 5th precinct parking lot when he was  approached by the owner of a stolen vehicle that had been recovered by police and returned to  him.  The owner wanted to return items located in the vehicle that did not belong to the owner,  one items of which was a cell phone    Officer Ketchmark informed the owner that they could dispose of the items, but the owner stated  that they'd feel more comfortable if the police took possession of the items    Officer Ketchmark took possession of the items and disposed of them.  When asked about the  phone during his investigative interview, Officer Ketchmark stated that the phone screen was  cracked and he was unable to turn on the phone or locate a serial number    The phone was later determined to have been stolen and when the owner attempted to recover  the phone, it was found that the phone had not been inventoried    The Police Conduct Review Panel (PCRP) returned a unanimous recommendation of Merit    MPD P&P 10­401(III)(C) addresses "Property Found During Work Assignment," and specifically cell  phones, which may be more appropriate than MPD P&P 10­401(IV)(B), which addresses  "Responsibility to Inventory Property and Evidence," as it is not clear that this phone would have  been considered, "evidence" at the time it was recovered    I agree with the PCRP that there is a preponderance of the evidence to support the allegation that  Officer Ketchmark failed to inventory property found during his work assignment.  This allegation is  Sustained      The facts noted in the case investigation support the conclusion that Officer Ketchmark violated  Minneapolis Police Department Policy 10­410. I concur with the recommendation from the Police Conduct  Review Panel that there is Merit to the allegation.  The allegation is Sustained.    As Chief of Police, I am responsible for providing clear expectations about behavior and conduct in all  situations, not just when it is convenient.  These expectations extend from myself to the command staff  and all levels of the Minneapolis Police Department.  My predecessors have also borne responsibility for  setting expectations, and it is incumbent on every Chief to hold officers accountable for breeches of those  expectations.  Legitimacy and reputation are of critical importance to police officers.  Officers carry a badge  and gun and are called upon to make some of the hardest decisions possible.  It takes countless actions to  build a reservoir of trust, and only one action to completely drain.  We cannot afford to lose legitimacy  with the people we serve.   DocuSign Envelope ID: 364B1AE1-D8E1-43DD-862A-F44B4D1DA9CA 1945822   I hold officers of the Minneapolis Police Department to a high standard.  I expect them to live up to our  oath of office, our professional code of ethics and our departments core values of trust, accountability and  professional service.  Furthermore, I expect them to demonstrate procedural justice in their work.  With  this conduct, Officer Ketchmark failed to meet our standards when he violated MPD P&P 10­410 during  this incident.  The violation in this matter undermines public trust.    As Chief of Police with authority to discipline for violations of policy under Minn. Stat 626.89 Subd. 17, I am  issuing a Letter of Reprimand to Officer Ketchmark for one of the policy violation listed below:     Allegation #1:  MPD P&P 10­410(IV)(B) – Sustained.  Letter of Reprimand           Brian O'Hara text: MINNEAPOLIS POLICE DEPARTMENT  Internal Affairs Unit  IAU Administrative Case #13-02  Sergeant Jon Hoff  CASE BACKGROUND  On January 12, 2013, Officer Renee Lewis contacted the desk officer at the 5th Precinct  and advised that she could not come to work because she was ill. Officer Lewis had been  coached (via a typed Word document) on two previous occasions by 5th Precinct  supervisors regarding the expectations for reporting to work and requesting time off.  Summary of Allegations  It is alleged that Officer Renee Lewis failed to comply with a lawful order given to her by  her supervisors about the expectations/procedures for calling in sick. It is alleged that  Officer Lewis left a message with a precinct desk officer advising that she needed a sick  day instead of contacting a supervisor as directed. It is also alleged that Officer Lewis  failed to report for work without proper authorization and was absent without leave  (AWOL).  If these allegations are found to be true, Officer Lewis could be in violation of the  following department policies:  1-403 COMPLIANCE WITH LAWFUL ORDERS  An employee must always obey lawful orders given by supervisors or their designees, but  all employees will be held accountable for their use of delegated authority.  An employee who receives conflicting lawful orders shall obey the most recent given.  After complying with the order, the employee should notify the next highest-ranking  employee above the employee giving the conflicting orders.  Employees shall truthfully answer questions or render material(s) and relevant statements  in a departmental investigation when so directed, consistent with the constitutional rights  of the employees.  1  1216440 3-303 ABSENT WITHOUT LEAVE  Employees are absent without leave (AWOL) when they fail to report for work or  abandon work or assignment without proper authorization.  If an investigation indicates that an AWOL violation occurred, an Internal Affairs  Complaint Form (MP-3401) shall be completed. A sustained finding requires that an  individual be marked "AWOL" for the day in question and lose a day of pay,  notwithstanding any other punitive measures. Should the day in question, as a result of  the findings, be marked "AWOL," MPD Human Resources personnel shall notify MPD  Payroll so that they may take appropriate action.  CASE INVESTIGATION  On January 12, 2013, 5th Precinct  13.43  Sergeant Kurt Radke sent an e-mail to  Internal Affairs Commander Arradondo regarding an officer's alleged policy violations  (see Radke e-mail tab). In the e-mail, Sergeant Radke explained that Officer Renee  Lewis was coached on two previous occasions regarding policy violations. Sergeant  Radke indicated that he believed the matter needed to be reviewed for formal discipline.  Sergeant Radke attached information regarding the past incidents with Officer Lewis.  Below is a summary of the three incidents referenced by Sergeant Radke.  January 12, 2013  Sergeant Radke provided a typed statement (no title) dated January 12, 2013, (see  January 12, 2013 tab). In this statement, Sergeant Radke wrote that he arrived for work  at 1915 hours on January 12, 2013, and was given a Post It note by Officer Jon Edwards  (5th Precinct desk officer at the time). Sergeant Radke said that Officer Edwards  informed him that Officer Renee Lewis called and said she was going to use a sick day.  Sergeant Radke attempted to call Officer Lewis after his 2000 hours roll call was  completed, but was unable to make contact with her.  Sergeant Radke noted that Officer Lewis had received coaching on two previous  occasions about the expectations of her notifying her supervisor directly if she wanted to  call in late or sick. He said that the coaching took place on July 22, 2012, and October  16, 2012. He believed that Officer Lewis was being insubordinate by disregarding the  coaching. Sergeant Radke believed that Officer Lewis was in violation of MPD Policy  and Procedure 1-403; Compliance with Lawful Order.  October 16, 2012  Sergeant Radke provided a typed statement with the title "Notice of Coaching" (see  October 16, 2012 tab). He wrote that on October 16, 2012, he and Lt. Loining spoke with  Officer Lewis about past incidents of Officer Lewis showing up late to work without  notifying a supervisor prior to her shift starting and taking a sick day without calling in  prior to her shift starting. text: 6/26/2021 Keefe case thrown out eight years after filed https://spokesman-recorder.com/2015/05/20/keefe-case-thrown-eight-years-filed/ 1/9 June 26, 2021        MAIN MENU Keefe case thrown out eight years after filed May 20, 2015  - by Eight years is a long time for a federal court to delay a decision (see Minneapolis Star Tribune headline, May 11, 2015: "Eighth circuit tosses out case alleging police corruption"). Why did it take them so long to get it so wrong, using strange claims, such as saying there was no "inhumane abuse of official power?" So what is "humane abuse of power?" Isn't abuse abuse? As we have documented for over a decade, there is still unsettled what Keefe calls "a massive case of corruption." Thus we are stunned that Minneapolis City Attorney Susan Segal still says that her office is "without any evidence" regarding Keefe's claims. In 2007, Lt. Michael Keefe was selected to command the Violent Offenders Task Force (VOTF) funded by federal and state funds. Keefe, a White man, discovered corruption in the VOTF Unit (such as overtime abuse ranging from $50-109 thousand of some officers, a statistically improbable arrest rate of 98 percent African Americans, etc.). Keefe was relieved of his VOTF command, despite high-command and high-performance ratings, and demoted to sergeant. Evidence: see the Star Tribune stories of February 2, 2008, April 6, 2011, September 2011, and its four-part 2009 series "The Informant," reporting on police department corruption, particularly part three, May 11, 2009, regarding informant Taylor Trump. Check out the April 22, 2009 front page        6/26/2021 Keefe case thrown out eight years after filed https://spokesman-recorder.com/2015/05/20/keefe-case-thrown-eight-years-filed/ 2/9 massive headline, "The Police vs. the Police." That four-part series raised a lot of questions that still await answers. Evidence: see our columns of August 29, 2007, November 26, 2008 and May 18, 2011. See also our extensive blog entry of December 14, 2007, about the five Black "Mill City 5" officers, who, with information provided by Michael Keefe, led the city to avoid a trial by offering a settlement of $750,000 for the five, which they accepted. All are still members of the MPD (two sergeants, two lieutenants, and one deputy chief). Evidence: the FBI reported the egregious resistance from White officers to work with Black officers, using the "N" word and other racially charged and derogatory terms about Black police officers, as well as confirming the significant overtime abuse and the 98 percent African American arrest number. Evidence: the five Black "Mill City" police officers made similar charges, including evidence supplied by Keefe. Rather than risk a trial, the city offered to settle for $750,000 settlement with those five officers. Evidence: Michael Keefe has put his job, his health, and his life on the line for justice. And so we again ask: Why was Keefe's case tossed out? Lt. Michael Keefe was not a man who was lying or making up issues when talking about racism and corruption in the city of Minneapolis and its police dept. Powerful forces can't stand anyone fighting or exposing racism and corruption. Some people say we need to move on, leave old problems behind and move on with the new ones. But aren't these old ones the new ones of today as well, as we see in Ferguson, New York City, Cleveland, Detroit, Baltimore, our own Minneapolis, etc.? Shouldn't officers like Michael Keefe be encouraged to stay and continue to serve rather than be pushed into retirement? The May 11, 2015 Star Tribune story gives the impression the claims Keefe brought are new, not eight years ago and still waiting answers. Instead his case is tossed aside in a most cavalier manner. We encourage readers review the four-part series and other statements and documentation referenced. How nervous does it leave you about tomorrow? text: 13.43 - Personnel Data said he did not see any other officers assisting officer Appledorn with  any arrests, but he thought he saw the booking van there.  When I asked 13.43 - Personnel Data if he remembered what the suspect looked like, that  Officer Appledorn was dealing with he said; "I think it was a guy. I think he was African  American".  13.43 - Personnel Data did remember  13.43  discussing Officer Appledom's report  with him. He said; "the only thing I can remember is just the force reporting part of it.  We talked about that briefly". 13.43 - Personnel Data said basically that  13.43  and he  talked about Officer Appledorn not doing a force report or that they thought she needed  to do a force report.  Statement of Officer• Sherry Appledorn  On June 03, 2010 at 1000 hrs, I interviewed Officer Appledorn at the Internal Affairs  office. Also in attendance was Officer Appledom's Federation Representative, Dan  Ungurian. I read Officer Appledorn Garrity and Tennessen Warnings and she said she  understood both.  Officer Appledom said on the morning of 04/25/10, she was outside Karma Night  club doing surveillance. She said she was wearing jeans, but she had on a MPD jacket,  with her duty belt on. She said it was obvious that she was a MPD police officer. She  said she was standing outside the night club, when she saw a man, (AP Ross) going  directly for one of the bouncers "and then just punched the bouncer". Officer Appledorn  said once she saw that happen, she ran up to AP Ross and grabbed him from behind.  Officer Appledom said the man was over six foot tall, with a heavy muscular build, so  it was hard for her when she was trying to pull him back. She said she couldn't pull the  8  1212293 AP back so "luckily another bouncer, who was a huge big guy, helped me pull him back  out of the crowd".  Officer Appledorn said when she got the AP back near the trunk of her car, with the  help of the bouncer; she tried to put handcuffs on AP Ross. She said Ross kept moving  and was not listening to her give him orders. She said with the bouncer helping her, she  was finally able to get API's hands behind his back and handcuffed. Officer Appledom  said once AP Ross was handcuffed, "The bouncer kind of just disappears on me and so  now I'm standing here left with this guy who's pissed mad and I'm asking him like three  or four times just to please just sit on the ground, you're under arrest, you're going to jail,  we have to wait for the booking van".  Officer Appledorn said she was trying to get Ross down to the ground by the squad,  because her vehicle was an unmarked and another squad near by had an arrested female  in the back seat, so she didn't want to put a male with a female. Officer Appledom said  she tried to get Ross "down just so nothing else happens with any other fights going on,  so he doesn't get injured. And I'm trying to move his feet to get him just to sit down and  he's not and he's pushing off the squad backing up into me at the same time I'm trying to  push him, bend him over".  Officer Appledom said AP Ross was pushing off the squad with his body, at the same  time he was backing his feet up and backing Officer Appledorn. Officer Appledorn said  she believed that the AP was trying to head butt, so at that time, "I open hand, gave him  just like an attention slap at the same time then I was able to get his, move his feet and  get him on the ground".  9  1212294 Officer Appledorn said she believed she slapped AP1 on the side of his cheek. She  said she did it from behind so there was "no full swing or anything it was just enough to  kind of hey, you know, you need to sit". Officer Appledorn said once she slapped AP1  she was able to get him to the ground.  When I asked Officer Appledorn if any other MPD officers helped her with AP Ross  besides the bouncer, she said; "Yeah, eventually when I got him on the ground then he  started, he started kicking and screaming and yelling on the ground and two other cops  came over and I don't know who they were and then  13.43 - Personnel Data  came over and  13.43 - Personnel Data was talking to him and then one cop was by his waist and one was on  his feet, holding his feet cause now he was kicking and kicking his, thrashing his feet all  about and kicking and at that time 13.43 was like go, go talk to the, I told him what  happened and then he was like go and so I went and talked to the victim, the bouncer".  Officer Appledorn said she believed  13.43  was also at her call, but she didn't  know. She said 13.43 - Personnel Data and the other officers, who she couldn't remember,  waited with AP Ross until the booking van came.  Officer Appledorn said she is no longer assigned to the 13.43 - Personnel Data Unit. She  said the day that she received her notification of allegation of misconduct from Internal  Affairs; she was transferred to  13.43 - Personnel Data  When I asked Officer Appledorn about her CAPRS supplement, (page 1 of 1),  specifically the part where she said, "he was already secured and handcuffed at this  point" and he pushed off the squad but that he wouldn't sit down so she slapped AP1 in  the face as a distraction measure.  10  1212295 I asked if he sat down when she slapped AP 1. She said; "Yeah I was able to give him  the, I mean I had to physically get him to sit down at the same time. It was, I couldn't get  his, I couldn't kick his feet out in order to take him down so I had to kind a, with  distracting him and then I had to kind of physically get him to sit in order to take him  down on the ground so he would sit there".  When I asked Officer Appledorn when she slapped AP1 in the face if she perceived  that as force. Officer Appledorn said she did not perceive a slap in the face as force. She  said it was a distraction technique and a way to get AP1 attention and get him to sit on  the ground. Officer Appledorn said she didn't do a force report because her actions were  not force.  When I asked Officer Appledorn if she still believed that she didn't use force, she  responded, "Correct, yes". When I showed Officer Appledorn the Use of Force policy  and showed her where it stated, "any physical strike to any part of the body of another"  and that a slap to the face would still be force, she replied; "Well I'm taking it as part of a  take down technique".  I then asked Officer Appledorn if she had ever had any defensive tactics training that  included slapping the face as a take down measure or distraction measure. Officer  Appledorn replied, "They talk about using open hand slaps as a way for distraction when  we do use of force, so".  Officer Appledorn remembered  13.43  speaking to her about her report. She  remembered  13.43  expressing concern that she did not do a use of force report, for  slapping AP Ross after he was handcuffed. Officer Appledorn said she told  13.43  11  1212296

text: MINNEAPOLIS POLICE DEPARTMENT  Internal Affairs Unit  Administrative Case #12-72  Sgt. D. N. Denno  Date 9-25-12  CASE BACKGROUND  On 5-3-12 employees at the Willman Trucking Co. 62 26 Av. N called 911 and requested  that a squad respond to the business because they had recovered three abandoned  shotguns. Willman Trucking is a business that provides heavy duty industrial size  dumpsters to other businesses who in turn fill them with scrap metal for disposal.  Willman delivers the dumpsters and then picks them up when the other business has  filled them and transports the metal to a scrap yard to be weighed and sold.  Willman keeps their inventory of dumpsters outside in the partially fenced yard. The  shotguns had been found when an employee was preparing a dumpster for delivery to a  customer. The dumpster had been next to a set of railroad tracks on the west side of the  yard and had partially filled with rain water. When the employee turned the dumpster to  empty the water the shotguns, wrapped in a sheet and quilted blanket, spilled out and  were identified. Employees had recalled seeing the blanket in the dumpster for at least 2  weeks, and probably longer than that, prior to 5-3-12.  1  1228089 Off. Brendon Schram and Off. Rickey Van Dyke (squad 410) were dispatched and  responded to the scene. According to an employee, Dave Fox, the officers took  possession of the three shotguns, placed them in the squad car trunk and left. Within  minutes the employees talked it over and called 911 to see if they could possibly get the  guns back if they were found to have not been stolen or used in a crime. They were put  in touch with the MPD Property and Evidence room and eventually found out the guns  had not been inventoried. The IAU Commander, Lt. Glampe was notified of the situation  by the Property and Evidence Director. An initial check of VisiNet records showed that  an entry had been made into the call from squad 410 that the guns were "replicas" and  had been left with the caller to dispose of.  CASE INVESTIGATION  VisiNet  Two VisiNet Reports were run in relation to this incident:  An Incident Detail Report was run (12-132495). This report shows the call received at  0907 hrs and includes details of the call. Those remarks read: "WILLMAN  TRUCKING/BACK OF BLDG SEE DAVE — FOUND 3 SHOTGUNS WARPPED  (misspelling in call) IN BLANKET IN AN UNUSED TANKER TRUCK DAVE WILL  KEEP AN EYE ON THEM UNTIL POLICE ARRIVE".  At 0929 hrs remarks were added by squad 410 that read: "are replicas found in trash  caller will dispose".  2  1228090 A Unit Activity Report for squad 410 was also run for 5-3-12. This report includes all  reported and dispatched activity for squad 410 for that day as well as Queries run from  the squad computer. As it pertains to this incident it shows the officers were dispatched  to 62 26 Av N at 09:09:44 hrs., arrived at 09:15:09 hrs and were again available for  another call at 09:29:37 hrs.  No Queries to check on firearms were made from squad 410 during their shift 5-3-12.  Many other queries on vehicles, persons and other property were made during the shift  and appear on this Activity Report.  The City Information Technology Dept. was contacted. At my request Marvin  Bachmeier checked squad 410's VisiNet activity for 5-3-12. He stated that if a query on  a firearm had been made from the squad computer it would appear in the Unit Activity  Report. He could not find any firearms queries made by squad 410 on 5-3-12.  Bachmeyer also explained the absence in the Activity Report of the added remark from  squad 410 about the guns being left with the caller. He said that added remarks to calls  are not placed on the Activity Report of the squad but are placed on the Incident Detail  Report for the specific call.  Lisa Madsen-Kaupang, Tracker/Business Technology Unit, also checked on firearms  queries made by the City of Minneapolis for the time frames surrounding the dispatched  call and for the time frame that Off. Van Dyke stated he made a second query from a 3rd Pct. computer terminal on 6-20-12.  3  1228091 The only firearms query made by Minneapolis on 5-3-12 between 0900 hrs and 1100 hrs  was not related to squad 410's call and was not made from squad 410's computer.  The record shows that on 6-20-12 between 0700 hrs and 1100 hrs there were 2 firearms  queries made by Minneapolis, both from a computer terminal at the Third Pct and  involving the firearms involved in this case. One of the queries entailed a check of serial  # A19825 which is the serial number on the Stevens Double barrel model 235.  Summary of interview with David Fox  David Fox is an employee at Willman Trucking. He was the employee that directed Off.  Schram and Off. Van Dyke to the guns. He was not the employee who had emptied the  tank and initially discovered the guns but he had taken responsibility to stay with the  guns until the police arrived and he turned the guns over to the officers. Mr. Fox was  interviewed at Willman Trucking on 5-15-12.  Mr. Fox said that on 5-3-12 at approximately 0900 hrs he was summoned to the rear of  the storage yard and found that another employee who was dumping water out of a  storage tank had found 3 shotguns wrapped in blankets. The police were called to pick  up the guns by the dispatcher in the office (Lisa Wilson). Mr. Fox stayed with the guns  and directed the officers to them.  Mr. Fox described the officers as being very distinctly different looking and from his  descriptions it was apparent that Off. Schram was the driver and Off. Van Dyke the  4  1228092 passenger. He said that the officers conducted function tests by dropping a pen down the  barrel and snapping the trigger on at least one of the guns.  Mr. Fox described the guns as being two double barrel shotguns with external hammers  and one pump shotgun with a large choke type device on the end of the barrel. He  remembered that one of the double barrels had a number possibly 237 or 235 stamped on  it and he believed a possible brand name beginning with the letter M, maybe McCallister  or McKenny. He had described all three guns as being in very poor condition and very  rusted probably due to the time spent in the water in the dumpster.  Mr. Fox said that he had some discussion with the officers about the final disposition of  the guns. He said the thinner officer said that if the guns were found to not be stolen or  used in a crime they would be "put in their showcase or whatever...". Mr. Fox said that  he was asked if he wanted the guns back and he said he wasn't interested in them.  Immediately after the officers left he said that he talked to Lisa (Wilson) and they  decided that other employees may be interested in having the guns back. She  immediately called 911 and began to inquire how to get the guns back if they were found  to be clear.  Mr. Fox said that the driver (Schram) put the pump shotgun in the squad car trunk and the  passenger (Van Dyke) put the two double barrels in the trunk.  5  1228093 David Fox Second Interview  On 7-2-12 Mr Fox was interviewed again after it was found that the guns had been  returned to Willman Trucking on 6-20-12.  Mr. Fox said that one of the officers who had responded to the initial call and taken the  shotguns returned them to Willman on 6-2-12. He had identified a Drivers License photo  of Off. Van Dyke as the officer who returned the guns on 6-20-12 . He said that the  officer was in plain clothes and was driving a black SUV with tinted windows. He said  he spoke briefly to Off. Van Dyke when he returned the guns. He also said that the guns  had since been taken home by other employees.  Summary of interview with Lisa Wilson  Lisa Wilson is the Dispatcher and Office Manager at Willman Trucking. She had been  notified by the workers in the yard on 5-3-12 that three shotguns had been found in one  of the dumpsters. She called 911 and requested that an officer be sent to recover the  guns. She said that she saw the squad car but did not see or have any interaction with the  officers that responded.  She said that Dave Fox had turned the guns over to the officers. Shortly after they left  Dave and Lisa began talking and they remembered that two other employees collected  guns. She immediately called 911 back and inquired as to whether they could have the  guns returned if they were found to be clear. She was put in touch with someone at the  6  1228094

text: A  I I egat io n 4: It is alleged that on 6/9/2010, Sergeant Ritschel, while working part-time at the Mar-Ten  Parking Garage, failed to complete a force report as related to the incident involving Dahl.  Investigative Facts:  •  Dahl has asserted that Ritschel pulled him off of his bike and forced him to the ground. In support of  his claim, Dahl provided me with photographs which according to him were taken 2 hours after the  incident. The photographs depict the injuries received as a result of the force used by Ritschel.  •  Ritschel stated that he did not use force on Dahl and instead asserts that Dahl had gotten off his bike  under his own power and in doing so lost his balance and fell to the ground. Ritschel claims to have  only handcuffed Dahl after he observed Dahl stand up with both hands clinched into a fist.  • The witness, Sarah Johansen, stated that she observed Ritschel push Dahl from his bike.  • There was no CAPRS force report found.  Relevant MPD Policy:  5-306 Use of Force — Reporting and Post Incident Requirements - Any sworn MPD employee that  uses force shall comply with the following requirements:  Medical Assistance:  As soon as reasonably practical, determine if anyone was injured and render medical aid consistent with  training and request Emergency Medical Service (EMS) if necessary.  Supervisor Notification and CAPRS Reporting Requirements  •  No CAPRS Report Required.  Unless an injury or alleged injury has occurred, the below listed force does not require a CAPRS report or  supervisor notification.  •  Escort Holds  •  Joint Manipulations  •  Nerve Pressure Points (Touch Pressure)  •  Handcuffing  •  Gun drawing or pointing  • CAPRS Report Required — No Supervisor Notification required.  The following listed force requires a CAPRS report, but does not require supervisor notification.  • Takedown Techniques  • Chemical Agent Exposure  • CAPRS Report Required - Supervisor Notification Required.  All other force, injuries or alleged injury incidents require both a CAPRS report and supervisor  notification. The sworn employee shall remain on scene and immediately notify a supervisor by phone or  radio of the force that was used.  9  1216263 •  A CAPRS report entitled "FORCE" shall be completed as soon as practical, but no later than the end  of that shift. A supplement describing the use of force incident in detail shall be completed and  entered directly into the CAPRS reporting system (no handwritten force reports). Employees shall  ensure that all applicable force portions of the CAPRS report are completed in full.  •  Sworn employees shall complete a CAPRS report entitled "PRIORI" for all incidents in which a  person has a prior injury, or prior alleged injury, and there is actual physical contact or transportation  by the police.  Transfer of Custody:  Prior to transferring custody of a subject that force was used upon. sworn MPD employees shall verbally  notify the receiving agency or employee of:  The type of force used,  •  Any injuries sustained (real or alleged) and  • Any medical aid / EMS rendered  Alleaation 5: It is alleged that on 6/9/2010 Sergeant Ritschel, while working part-time at the Mar-Ten.  Parking Garage, failed to inform MECC (Channel 7) that he was going to be working in the capacity of a  part-time officer.  Investigative Facts:  • According to VisiNet logs, Ritschel was not signed in to work on 6/9/2010.  •  No MECC phone calls and/or radio traffic were found on 6/9/2010 that indicated Ritschel signed on  as a part-time officer.  Relevant ISTPD Policy:  3-809 Off-Duty Employment Communications/Call Signs/Radio Usage - For officer safety, all  officers working off-duty shall have portable radio with them and stay in contact with the appropriate  dispatch channel.  All MPD officers working off-duty employment shall contact Channel 7 and provide their name, badge  number, hours of off-duty employment, location of the off-duty work site and obtain off-duty call sign.  Channel 7 maintains list of call signs for specific off-duty locations and those assigned call signs shall be  used.  If the off-duty employment is at a location that does not have a permanent call sign, Channel 7 will assign  a call sign for that location. All officer shall contact Channel 7 at the end of their off-duty shift to log off.  If an officer needs to use the radio during the course of working an off-duty job, the officer shall preface  the transmission with "OFF-DUTY" followed by the call sign.  *  *  *  10  1216264 Allegation 6:  It is alleged that on September 10, 2010 Sergeant Ritschel intentionally made false  statements during a formal statement, taken under Garrity as related to IA 10-165. These statements are  specifically related to:  •  Allegation 2 — It is alleged that on June 9, 2010, Sergeant Ritschel, while working part- time at the Mar-Ten Parking Garage, used unreasonable and unnecessary force when he  pulled Dahl from his bike, forced him to the ground, and placed him in handcuffs. As a  result of this force, Dahl asserts that he sustained cuts to his knees and wrist's.  •  Alleilation 3: It is alleged that on 6/9/2010 Sergeant Ritschel, while working part-time at  the Mar-Ten Parking Garage, failed to complete a CAPRS report after arresting Dahl  and issuing him a citation for: Fail to Obey Police Officer; MN §169.02s2 and Fail to  Change Address on DL; MN §171.11.  Investigative Facts:  Related to the issue of force;  •  Dahl stated that Ritschel used force while Ritschel maintains that he did not use force.  •  The witness, Sarah Johansen, described a scenario in which Ritschel did use force in that Ritschel was  observed by Johansen to have pushed Dahl from his bike.  •  Dahl produced photographs that he stated were taken 2 hours after the incident. The images are  meant to depict the injuries received as a result of the force used by Ritschel.  • Johansen stated to the MECC operator that it appeared as though Dahl had scrapped his knee.  • There are no video images known to exist that would show evidence either way as to what transpired  between Ritschel and Dahl.  Related to the issue of a completed CAPRS report;  •  Ritschel stated that he had completed a CAPRS report on June 14, 2010, as related to the incident that  occurred on June 9, 2010. Ritschel also stated that this report had been done on a City of Minneapolis  computer situated in the licensing unit.  •  According to the City of Minneapolis — City Attorney's Office, case management system the citation  written to Dahl (1100027406) was dismissed on July 29, 2010 based on the fact that there was no  report completed by Ritschel.  •  Prior to conducting the formal interview with Ritschel, I conducted a query of the CAPRS database  using Dahl's name as well as Ritschel's badge number. These queries produced similar results in that  no report was found to have been completed by Ritschel as related to this incident.  • An audit of Ritschel's City of Minneapolis computer account for June 9, 2010 and June 14, 2010,  revealed no evidence that he completed a CAPRS report associated with this incident.  11  1216265 Relevant MPD Policy  2-106 Complaint Investigations — Garrity Decision - MPD employees are required to give a statement  when ordered to do so regarding matters pertaining to the scope of their employment and their fitness for  duty. These statements or the fruits thereof, compelled as a condition of employment, cannot be then used  in any criminal proceedings against the employee, except in cases of alleged perjury by the employee  giving the statement (Garrity vs. New Jersey, 1967, U.S. Supreme Court). (01/15/08)  All employees shall answer all questions truthfully and fully render material and relevant statements to a  competent authority in an MPD investigation when compelled by a representative of the Employer,  consistent with the constitutional rights of the individuals. (09/19/08)  All statements of involved police employees shall be signed and sworn. Any employee found to have  intentionally given a false statement shall be subject to MPD disciplinary procedures, up to and including  dismissal.  Criminal violations will be referred to the appropriate prosecuting authority for review. The  administrative case involving alleged policy and procedure violations may proceed independent of the  criminal case.  Res ectfullys bm ted.  Sergeant Jason Case  Internal Affairs Unit  12  1216266

Melissa Chiodo  May 25, 2011  CASE BACKGROUND  On 03/23/2011, Sgt. Stichter forwarded a Coaching Document to Internal Affairs  in an effort to document the incident and notify IAU, it was not meant to document  coaching. The involved employee in the coaching document is Officer Patrick Reuben.  According to the Coaching Document, at approximately 1800 hours on 03/23/2011 Linda  Cushing, a civilian in the Traffic Unit was downtown with her husband Thomas and two  Juvenile foreign exchange students and she gave them a tour of the Traffic Office where  she works. During the tour, Officer Reuben who was in the roll call room, talked with  the two students, and handcuffed them and Linda Cushing took photos.  One of the students, Yuliya Yegerova, a 17-year-old female from Russia, was  asking about equipment on Officer Reuben's belt and asked about his service weapon.  Officer Reuben handed his gun to Yuliya and she allegedly pointed it at her head and  began laughing. Officer Reuben took the gun away and Linda told Officer Reuben that it  was not funny and to put the gun away. Several hours later Officer Reuben told his  supervisor about the incident.  1  1212227 On 03/24/2011, Lt. Glampe notified Chief Gerlicher after we received the  Coaching Document detailing this incident.  CASE INVESTIGATION  I interviewed all of the police officers that were present during any part of the  incident, which included, Officer Reuben, Officer Pfaff, and Officer Indehar. I also  interviewed Sgt. Stichter since he submitted the coaching document and was the Traffic  Unit Supervisor working the night of the incident and Officer Reuben had reported it to  him. I interviewed Linda Cushing; she was in the roll call room and saw part of the  incident. Linda Cushing asked her husband Tom if he would be willing to give a  statement to Internal Affairs since he was present during the event and he declined due to  his current health concerns.  Linda advised me that neither of the exchange students wanted to give a  statement, the girls were both extremely upset by the incident, and they wanted to put it  behind them. Since they were minors, their parents were not in the country, and they  would be leaving the United States in a short period of time I did not contact the girls.  The week of May 2, 2011 Bonnie Knoll, the host parent to Yuliya Yegerova  contacted me and said Yuliya wanted to speak to me. Bonnie said that Yuliya would like  to give a statement regarding what happened in the Traffic Unit Office on March 23,  2011 but she was not comfortable going to a police facility after the gun incident. I  agreed to conduct an interview off-site at a Coffee Shop in Blaine near the Knoll's home.  Yuliya is a minor and from a foreign country so I allowed her host parents Tom and  2  1212228 Bonnie Knoll to be present during the interview. Yuliya will be returning to Russia on  May 25, 2011.  Hanna-Kaisa Mustasilta is a 17-year-old exchange student from Finland who was  the other exchange student present during the incident on March 23, 2011. Linda  Cushing relayed to me that Hanna still did not want to talk about the incident. Since  Hanna did not want to talk, is a minor, and would be returning to Finland in early May, I  did not contact her for an interview.  CASE STATEMENTS  Linda Cushing Statement  Linda said that her and her husband brought two exchange students to the Traffic  Office and the Haaf Ramp. The girls sat in a squad, she took a picture and then went into  the roll call room where she introduced them to officers, and she asked if anyone would  be willing to hand cuff the girls and Patrick said he would. Patrick handcuffed the girls  and she took a couple of photos. They were getting ready to leave when she looked over  and saw Yuliya holding a gun and in her hand and then point it at her head. Linda said  she was like "Oh my God" she grabbed the girls and they left.  Linda said that when this happened Officer Pfaff was still in the roll call room but  she was not sure if Officer Indehar was still in the room.  Linda said she did not hear a gun racking, as if someone was ejecting a shell or  unloading a gun. She said she has heard that sound before at work. She said if she would  have a heard a sound like that she might have turned her head to see what was going on.  Linda said she has seen a gun-clearing barrel before but there is not one in the roll call  3  1212229

text: Allegation #5: Sergeant David Robinson  It is alleged that on Tuesday January 18, 2011 at approximately 1815 hours, Sergeant David  Robinson did not fulfill the responsibilities of an on-duty supervisor regarding the reporting of  force used during an incident regarding MPLS CAPRS Report 11-016025. If this allegation  were true, it would violate the following:  5-307 SUPERVISOR FORCE REVIEW (08/17/07) (12/15/09)  On-duty Supervisor Responsibilities.  The supervisor who is notified of a Use of Force incident by any sworn MPD employee shall:  1. Instruct the involved employees to have the subject of the use of force remain on-scene until the  supervisor arrives, if it is reasonable to do so.  a. If the subject of the use of force does not remain on-scene, the supervisor shall go to the subject's  location, if necessary, to complete the investigation.  2. Respond to the incident scene and conduct a preliminary investigation of the Use of Force incident.  a. Determine if the incident meets the criteria for a Critical Incident.  • If so, follow Critical Incident Policy, Section 7-810.  b. Debrief the employee(s) who engaged in the use of force.  c. Note any reported injury (actual or alleged) to any individual involved.  d. Photograph any visible signs of injury to any individual involved.  e. Note any medical aid/EMS rendered to any individual involved.  f. Locate and review any evidence related to the force incident (e.g. MVR, security video, private  cameras, etc.). (12/15/09)  g. Ensure any on-scene evidence is preserved and collected.  h. Locate and identify witnesses to the use of force incident. (12/15/09)  i. Obtain statements from witnesses to the use of force incident.  j. Contact the Internal Affairs Unit Commander immediately by phone if the force used appears to be  unreasonable or constitutes possible misconduct.  3. Complete and submit the Supervisor Use of Force Review and Summary in CAPRS as soon as  practical, but prior to the end of that shift.  a. Ensure that all actions taken in the preliminary investigation process and the information obtained from  these actions are included in the Summary and that all other relevant information is entered in the  appropriate sections of the report. (12/15/09)  b. Include in the Summary, a specific statement that indicates based upon the totality of information  available at the time of report, whether it is believed that the force used was: (12/15/09)  • reasonable and within policy; or  • not reasonable or not within policy  4. Review all sworn employees' CAPRS reports and supplements related to  the use of force incident for completeness and accuracy.  27  1210161 Investigative Facts   On Tuesday January 18, 2011 at approximately 1815 hours, Sergeant David Robinson  was the on-duty supervisor responsible for the incident regarding MPLS CAPRS Report  11-016025.5°  Sergeant Robinson spoke to each individual officer that reported the use of force during  the incident regarding MPLS CAPRS Report 11-016025.51  Officers Brandon Bartholomew, 13.43 - Personnel Data  kicking AP2/Page during the apprehension process. 2 and 13.43 - Personnel Data all reported   Officers Bartholomew, 13.43 13.43 and 13.43 described AP2/Page's actions as  resisting officers' efforts to gain control of him. Page was not actively fighting or using  deadly force against officers.  Officers based their force against AP2/Page on  information from a CRI that Page was seen with a gun during a controlled buy, Page was  reaching toward his waistband and Page was not complying with officers' verbal  commands to show his hands.53  Officers did not observe AP2/Page with a gun and / or dangerous weapon during this  incident. Officers did not recover a gun and / or dangerous weapon from AP2/Page or  the route in which Page traveled during the brief foot pursuit.54  Administrative Announcement 09-058 (AA09-058) states: Punches, kicks or higher  levels of force are tactics that are acceptable when suspects are fighting with officers or  when there is a legitimate, articulable threat to officer safety. Punches, kicks or higher  levels of force are not appropriate when:  1. Used on defensively resistant prone subjects absent other factors;  2. Used against persons who are passively resisting;  3. Used to simply gain compliance of a defensively resistant prone  subject without attempts at lesser levels of control; and  a. When lesser levels of control are available;  b. When there are multiple officers present.55   AP2/Page sustained significant injuries to his face/head because of force used by  officers.56 5° Robinson Statement Pg. 2 Lines 14-18  51 Robinson Statement Pg. 2 Lines 20-22  52 MPLS CAPRS Report 11-016025 (FORCE Reports I, 3 & 4)  53 Robinson Statement Pg. 3 Lines 12-36  54 MPLS CAPRS Report 11-016025 all supplements / Statements of Bartholomew,  Suchta  55 Administrative Announcement 09-058 (AA09-058)  56 MPLS CAPRS Report 11-016025 (FORCE Reports 1, 3 & 4)  13.43  13.43 and  28  1210162  Sergeant Robinson observed the injuries sustained by AP2/Page because of force used  by MPD officers. Sergeant Robinson did not think that AP2/Page required medical  attention. Sergeant Robinson described AP2/Page's injuries as bumps, bruises and  swelling. The injuries appeared to look bad but did not appear to be broke open or  require a stitch. Sergeant Robinson did not believe that Page needed anything more than  an observation.57  Upon entry into the Hennepin County Jail (HCJ), HCJ staff immediately requested the  HCJ nurse to evaluate AP2/Page prior to entry. HCJ nurse observed and evaluated  AP2/Page's injuries. HCJ nurse refused AP2/Page entry into HCJ without prior medical  treatment from Hennepin County Medical Center (HCMC).58  Ser cant Robinson did not review Officers Bartholomew, 13.43  13.43 and  13.43. Review.  , their supplemental reports prior to completing his Supervisor's Force  13.43 - Personnel Data  57 Robinson Statement Pg. 5 Lines 44 — 46 & Pg. 6 Lines 1-7  58 MPLS CAPRS Report 11-016025 Supplements 5 & 6 / HCJ Sally Port Video  59 Robinson Statement Pg. 6 Lines 9-20  29  1210163 SUMMARY  Regarding allegations 1, 2, and 3, Officers Bartholomew, 13.43 and  used force that appeared to be inappropriate against AP2/Page durin  CAPRS Report 11-016025. After reviewing Officers Bartholomew,  and Suchta, their supplemental reports #'s 7 5 2, 6 and 1 respectively, the kicking force used by  Officers Bartholomew, 13.43 and 13.43 appeared to be unreasonable.  13.43 respectively  the incident involvin  Officers Bartholomew, 13.43 and 13.43 reported that they made the decision to use the  force that they used against AP2/Page based on information from a CRI, that the CRI observed  an alleged gun in the waistband of AP2/Page during two different controlled buys. Officers  stated that Page was digging in his waistband during the brief foot pursuit but described Page's  clothing as baggie and loose fitting. Officers stated that when Page was lying on the ground, he  continued to reach for his waistband, kick his legs and swing his arms.  Officers' descriptions of AP2/Page's action were resisting officers' attempts to gain control of  Page. Page was not aggressively fighting officers. Page was lying on the ground on his  stomach. Page was pulling away form officers. Page was trying to roll over. Page was kicking  his feet and swinging his arms while lying on the ground on his stomach.  Officers need to maintain their safety using information that they obtain to heighten their  awareness. Officers' actions against AP2/Page should have been based on dangers that existed  at the time and not assumptions and possibilities. With five officers present and AP2/Page lying  on the ground on his stomach with no apparent dan_ers resent  13.43 respectively  AP2/Page in the head,  body and groin by Officers Bartholomew, 13.43 and  3  respectively appeared to be  unreasonable.  13.43 - Personnel Data  Regarding allegation 5, Sergeant Robinson must have the capabili  of Imderstandinwhen  force appears to be unreasonable.  When Officers Bartholomew, 1 3 .43 and 13.43 informed Sergeant Robinson that they kicked AP2/Page in his head, body and groin  respectively, it is prudent for Sergeant Robinson to ensure that probable cause currently existed  during the use of force by officers. Officers' assumptions and possibilities do not create  Probable cause.  13.43 - Personnel Data  Respectfully  Montg  Internal Aff  nit  31  1210165

Kara Peterson  April 9, 2013  CASE BACKGROUND  This case was forwarded to the Minneapolis Police Department Internal Affairs Unit  by Driver and Vehicle Services. In a Driver and Vehicle Services (DVS) audit, it was  determined that Officer James Golgart's login and password had been used to query  18 USC 2721 & 2725  s data two times on February 16th,  2013. The queries were conducted at 1809 hours and 2242 hours.  13.43 - Personnel Data  13.43  CASE INVESTIGATION  This investigator reviewed audit information provided by Kim Jacobson, Data  Practices Coordinator with Driver and Vehicle Services. According to the audit,  s DVS data was queried at 1809 hours and 2242 hours by  18 USC 2721 & 2725  Officer James Golgart on February 16th, 2013. A different IP address was associated  with each query.  1  1214048 According to the Minneapolis Police Department Work Force Director Database,  Officer Golgart was assigned to 5th Precinct 13.43  from 2000 hours to 0600  hours on February 16th, 2013 .  Statement of Officer James Golgart  Officer James Golgart provided a statement to this investigator on April 2", 2013, in the  Internal Affairs Unit office. Officer Golgart is currently assigned to Fifth Precinct  13.43  Officer Golgart was advised that the purpose of his statement related to an administrative  review regarding his alleged Driver and Vehicle Services access of  18 USC 2721 & 2725  Officer Golgart stated that  18 USC 2721 & 2725  's personal data on February 16th, 2013.  13.43  18 USC 2721 & 2725  13.43  13.43 - Personnel Data  Officer Golgart confirmed that he worked a 13.43  shift on February 16th, 2013, from  2000 hours to 0600 hours. Officer Golgart confirmed that he did query  in DVS at 1809 hours and 2242 hours on February 16th, 2013.  2  1214049 When asked if the DVS access of 18 USC 2721 & 2725 's data was related to law enforcement  duties with the Minneapolis Police Department, Officer Golgart stated:  13.43 - Personnel Data  Officer Golgart stated that when he queried the data, he confirmed that it was the  individual he knew, closed out DVS, and did not use the data in any other matter.  SUMMARY OF ALLEGATIONS  Allegation # 1: It is alleged that Officer James Golgart accessed the DVS records of  18 USC 2721 & 2725 without an official business-related purpose.
